quotes.verbatim_quote:
the cycle in which we are most interested is from dominant V to tonic I. Now
the question is, what is the V and the I? The V of a chord demands resolution, the I is
final. In other words, once you have played the | the ear is satisfied.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the fundamental harmonic tension-and-release relationship between dominant and tonic that underpins all subsequent cycle work in the method.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Any chord that you
play during the course of a cycle demands some kind of temporary resolution until it
eventually reaches the V (dominant the most active chord of the tonality) and the V re-
solves into the I
quotes.why_load_bearing: This establishes the hierarchical role of the dominant within a chord cycle, explaining why all harmonic motion gravitates toward V before resolving to I.

quotes.verbatim_quote: One of the most popular cycles is from I to IV to V and back to 1
quotes.why_load_bearing: This names and prioritizes the I-IV-V-I progression as a foundational cycle students must internalize before advancing.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Another popular example, and the one in which we are most interested, is from I to II ta
Vv to I
quotes.why_load_bearing: This flags the I-II-V-I cycle as the primary focus of the lesson, setting up the substitution concept introduced in lesson 9.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Commit these exercises to memory because in lesson 9 we will approach the art of sub-
stituting minor II chords for dominant V chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is a forward-looking rule that assigns pedagogical weight to memorizing the cycles and directly motivates the substitution technique to be taught next.
